title: Are Analyst “Top Picks” Informative?
abstract: Following the Global Settlement, analysts extensively use a top pick designation allowing for greater granularity of information among buy recommended stocks, but conflicts of interest can potentially influence this designation. Examining a novel sample of top picks, we find that a calendar-time portfolio of top picks generates an abnormal performance of 17.6% per year. Top picks have greater investment value than do buy recommendations and alternative analyst investment strategies. Both institutional and retail investors trade in response to top picks. However, only institutional investors appear to identify top picks that have greater investment value when they are announced.

author.Justin Birru: Fisher College of Business, The Ohio State University,USA
author.Sinan Gokkaya: Phil and Pat Muck College of Business, Ohio University,USA
author.Xi Liu: Farmer School of Business, Miami University,USA
author.René Stulz*: Fisher College of Business, The Ohio State University,USA, NBER, and ECGI

article.1 Institutional Background, Sample, and:
JEL G11 - Portfolio Choice; Investment Decisions G12 - Asset Pricing; Trading volume; Bond Interest Rates G14 - Information and Market Efficiency; Event Studies; Insider Trading G20 - General G23 - Non-bank Financial Institutions; Financial Instruments; Institutional Investors G24 - Investment Banking; Venture Capital; Brokerage; Ratings and Ratings Agencies Issue Section: Article Editor: Lauren Cohen Lauren Cohen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

In the early 2000s, concerns about conflicts of interest of sell-side analysts led to new regulations and eventually to the Global Analyst Research Settlement. As discussed inKadan et al. (2009), one important by-product of these regulations is the adoption of a new three-tier coarser stock rating system by most leading investment banks.1Before the Global Settlement, 85% of analyst stock recommendations are issued using a traditional five-tier rating system, but only less than 20% are afterward.

Though a coarser three-tier rating system has the potential to reduce gains to analysts from engaging in strategic behavior relative to a five-tier rating system, such a system also reduces the information available to investors. That is, sell-side analysts cannot discriminate between stocks whose performance they expect to be good and those whose performance they expect to be the best. Given that institutional investors (e.g., mutual funds, pension funds, endowments), the primary consumers of analyst research, are typically long-only investors, they can purchase any buy recommended stock but can benefit from sell recommendations mostly for their existing positions. Therefore, institutional investors value buy recommendations more than sell recommendations (e.g.,Groysberg et al. 2013), and we would expect brokerage houses to attempt to increase granularity among buy recommended stocks to mitigate the impact of the new coarser three-tier stock rating system. Consistently, we show that a new stock designation, “top pick,” emerges following the Global Analyst Research Settlement and its use becomes widespread among brokers that transitioned from a five-tier to three-tier rating system.

An analyst’s top pick is defined as the analyst’s best investment idea. Notwithstanding the disproportionate amount of attention top picks receive from regulatory agencies, the media, and investors, there exists no academic research on top picks that we are aware of.2A possible reason for this lack of research is that I/B/E/S does not distinguish between top picks and buy recommendations and treats all post-Global Settlement buy recommendations the same. As a result, little is known about even the basic details of top picks, the investment value of top picks, and whether analysts use top pick designations to give their best investment advice to investors or use these designations to pursue strategic objectives that are not in the best interest of investors. Exploiting a novel sample of 3,563 top picks by 113 unique brokerage houses over 1999-2016, we investigate whether top picks have investment value for investors, whether that investment value is diminished by potential conflicts of interest, and whether institutional and retail investors both trade on top picks.

Top picks differ from stock recommendations in several distinct ways. First, top pick is not a stock recommendation category but instead an optional designation that represents an analyst’s singlebestidea within her coverage universe. In contrast, a buy recommendation typically means a stock is expected to outperform its industry peers. Hence, while there can be multiple buy-rated stocks in an analyst’s coverage portfolio, there can be at most one stock with a top pick designation by an analyst in a given year according to industry practice. Second, unlike stock recommendations, top pick designations are assigned to a stock only for the upcoming one-year investment horizon and are announced typically at the end or beginning of the year. Top picks almost always expire on December 31st. Third, though analysts select their top pick stock from their buy recommendations, only 19% of top pick announcements coincide with a stock recommendation initiation, reiteration, or revision. In other words, the announcement of a top pick designation represents a stand-alone analyst research output, and we can directly assess its investment value as well as its impact on investor trading behavior. Fourth, top picks appear to be used as a marketing tool for brokerage houses. Indeed, a primary reason for the clustering of top pick announcements from November through February is so that brokers can advertise stocks as their top picks for the upcoming calendar year. In addition, brokers frequently showcase their top pick selections to institutional clients at best idea conferences. In contrast, brokers do not follow such a practice for stock recommendations.

To make the distinction between top picks and analyst stock recommendations clearer, it is useful to consider an example. On December 17, 2012, an analyst from Barclays announced Penn National Gaming (henceforth, Penn) as her top pick for 2013 without revising or reiterating her stock recommendation, target price, or earnings-per-share (EPS) forecasts. At the time of this top pick announcement, her coverage portfolio consists of eight firms,3with five of the eight coverage stocks having a buy recommendation. Following the sell-side research industry convention, Barclays defines a top pick as “the single best alpha-generating investment idea within each industry and a top pick is taken from among the Overweight-rated stocks within that industry.” I/B/E/S records neither the announcement of the top pick nor the top pick designation. According to I/B/E/S, the analyst from Barclays announced a buy recommendation for Penn on July 27, 2012, and Penn is recorded only as a “buy” recommendation.4

Our unique database allows us to provide evidence about the use of top picks over time and about the determinants of the top pick designation. We first show that top picks become common after the regulatory changes of 2002. We find almost no top pick designation before 2002. In contrast, from 2005 to 2016, there are 278 top picks on average per year, and 87% of the top picks are generated by brokerages that switched from the five-tier rating scale to a coarser three-tier rating scale. On average, top picks tend to be larger growth stocks with higher institutional ownership. Top pick stocks have better past performance, lower idiosyncratic volatility and lower analyst dispersion. As discussed earlier, the move to a three-tier rating system was intended to reduce the importance of potential conflicts of interest, but top picks reintroduce granularity among buy ratings that could be used by analysts to pursue strategic objectives. Though there is evidence that investment-banking-related conflicts of interest have become less important after the passage of the Global Settlement (e.g.,Kadan et al. 2009;Corwin, Larocque, and Stegemoller 2017), post-Settlement period analyst compensation continues to be based on overall revenue and profitability of an analyst’s firm, including that of the investment banking unit. Further, the limited survey evidence available about post-Settlement analyst research behavior shows that institutional investors continue to view analyst research to be affected by investment-banking-related conflicts of interests and analysts view their success in generating underwriting business for affiliated investment banking units as very important to their compensation (Brown et al. 2015). We find that a brokerage house’s investment banking clients are more likely to be designated as top picks, which heightens the concern about potential conflicts of interest in the designation of top picks. Finally, we find that stocks receiving a top pick designation are stocks for which analysts are more optimistic with respect to their earnings forecasts and target price implied returns.

We find strong evidence that top pick designations have superior investment value for investors. For instance, a calendar-time portfolio strategy that invests in analyst top picks one day after they are announced and holds these top picks while they remain in force earns roughly a 1.33% characteristic-adjusted (Daniel et al. 1997, henceforth DGTW) monthly average abnormal return (17.18% in annual terms). In contrast, a strategy of investing in buy recommendations by the same analyst during the same year earns only 0.51% (6.29% in annual terms). We find consistent results from a panel regression methodology that controls for a host of analyst-, broker-, and firm-specific characteristics, and when measuring abnormal stock returns with alternative risk adjustments. These results suggest that analysts exhibit skill in identifying their best investment ideas and potential strategic motives do not rob top picks of their investment value, on average. In other words, analyses of analyst stock-picking skill that ignores top picks likely understate the stock-picking ability of analysts in the post-Settlement period. Next, we compare the investment value of top picks to that of buy recommendations in the same industry during the same year and find that DGTW-adjusted monthly returns of top picks are 90 basis points higher than those of industry-year-matched buy recommendations. This finding suggests that top picks also represent the best ideas in an industry and one should consider a stock’s top pick designation when analyzing the information content ofallstock recommendations in the post-regulatory period.5

Many alternative approaches to construct investment portfolios that exploit analyst research have been proposed in the literature. Therefore, we compare the investment performance of the top pick portfolio strategy to the investment performance of alternative calendar-time portfolios formed using (a) stocks with the most favorable consensus recommendation level (Barber et al. 2001), (b) stocks upgraded to the most favorable recommendation level (Barber et al. 2001), (c) stocks with the most favorable consensus recommendation level or upgraded to the most favorable recommendation level and with the highest consensus target price implied stock return (Huang, Mian, and Sankaraguruswamy 2009), and (d) stocks that are upgraded to the most favorable recommendation level from brokers with the least favorable recommendation distribution or from independent brokers (Barber et al. 2006) or buy recommendations from analysts with superior relative earnings forecast performance (Loh and Mian 2006). Examining both calendar-time portfolio strategies and panel regressions, we find that the investment performance of top picks is much stronger than the investment performance of these alternative analyst investment strategies.

After demonstrating that top picks have investment value, we investigate the cross-sectional determinants of top picks’ investment performance. First, we find no evidence that the investment value of top picks designated on investment banking clients is lower than that of top picks on nonclient firms. Second, we consider whether the investment value of top pick designations is related to the forecasting optimism of an analyst for the top pick stock. If forecasting optimism reflects behavioral biases (e.g.,Cen, Hilary, and Wei 2013;Jannati et al. 2022) or strategic considerations, we would expect the investment value of top picks to be negatively related to analyst forecasting optimism. Alternatively, if forecasting optimism reflects an analyst’s genuine expectation of future stock outperformance of the top pick stock and analysts possess stock-picking skill, top picks accompanied by more optimistic forecasts are expected to generate even greater abnormal returns relative to other top picks. Our evidence is consistent with the latter view. Since we interpret our results as evidence of analyst stock-picking ability, we would also expect top picks from analysts with better reputations and longer forecasting experience to perform better. Consistently, we find that the investment performance of top picks is greater for analysts with All-Star status as well as with longer general and firm-specific forecasting experience. Lastly, we find that analysts at brokerages with greater resources do not appear to have top picks with better investment performance.

So far, we have shown that top picks have investment value and that potential strategic motives for top pick designations do not adversely affect the investment value of top picks. Next, we turn our attention to identifying which investors trade on top picks. We first investigate whether institutional and retail investors trade in response to top pick announcements. Examining institutional daily trading imbalances with equity transactions obtained fromAncerno Ltdand retail trading imbalance withTrade and Quote (TAQ)data (e.g.,Boehmer et al. 2021), we find that both institutional and retail investors exhibit more pronounced buying behavior following the announcement of top picks relative to buy recommendations. Next, we explore institutional and retail investors’ ability to differentiate among top picks based on their investment value when top picks are announced. Our findings show that top pick announcements accompanied by a higher institutional trading imbalance exhibit higher investment value than an average top pick, suggesting that institutions identify top picks that have greater investment value. In contrast, retail investor trading behavior is not correlated with top pick investment performance. It follows from this that institutional investors appear to identify top picks that have greater investment value when they are announced while retail investors do not.

Our paper contributes to multiple segments of the literature focused on sell-side analysts and their stock recommendations. First, we contribute to the vast body of analyst literature attempting to identify the most influential stock recommendations and assess sell-side analysts’ stock-picking skills based on individual analyst or brokerage house characteristics (see, for instance,Stickel 1992;Clement 1999;Asquith, Mikhail, and Au 2005;Bae, Stulz, and Tan 2008), stock-level abnormal returns, and the state of the economy (Loh and Stulz 2011,2018). Our results suggest that top pick designations need to be taken into account when evaluating the role of analysts and their stock-picking skill in the post-regulatory period. Prior research has documented that analyst outputs, such as consensus stock recommendation levels, revisions to the most favorable recommendation level, consensus target price implied stock returns, brokerage houses’ stock recommendation distribution and independence from investment banking units, as well as analysts’ relative earnings forecasting performance, can be used to generate significant abnormal results (Barber et al. 2001,2006;Loh and Mian 2006;Huang, Mian, and Sankaraguruswamy 2009). We show that an investment strategy solely based on top pick designations generates superior investment returns compared to alternative analyst investment strategies considered in prior research.

Second, we add to the literature that seeks to understand the implications of the regulatory environment on sell-side research and potential conflicts of interest emanating from investment banking business. Buy (hold/sell) stock recommendations have become less (more) common following the Global Analyst Research Settlement (e.g.,Barber et al. 2006;Clarke et al. 2011), and there is evidence of a reduction or even elimination of investment-banking-related strategic behavior (Kadan et al. 2009;Corwin et al. 2017).Kadan et al. (2009)also show that most investment banks transition from a traditional five-tier rating system to a coarser three-tier rating system in the post-Settlement period. We add to this literature by documenting that brokers transitioning to a three-tier rating system adopt a new “top pick” designation that adds granularity to buy recommended stocks to mitigate the impact of the new coarser three-tier stock rating system. Though top pick designations may at times be affected by strategic considerations, these considerations do not appear to adversely affect the investment performance of top picks on average.

Third, we contribute to the literature that examines whether institutional investors can sort through Wall-Street research and discern between good and bad stock recommendations. For example,Malmendier and Shanthikumar (2007),Mikhail, Walther, and Willis (2007), and others find that institutions differentiate among stock recommendations in their trading. In contrast,Busse, Green, and Jegadeesh (2012)do not find evidence that institutions can differentiate among analysts’ stock recommendations. Exploiting the unique and important laboratory provided by analysts’ top picks, we revisit this research question and document that institutions discern among top picks based on their investment value when top picks are announced.
In 2002, the NYSE adopted Rule 472, NASD adopted Rule 2711, and 10 of the largest U.S. investment firms entered an enforcement agreement with the SEC, the NASD, and the NYSE to address investment-banking-related potential conflicts of interest concerning stock recommendations by sell-side analysts. Regulators believed that these potential conflicts of interest led analysts to make too optimistic stock recommendation decisions for strategic reasons, such as helping their firm’s investment banking arm win future mandates. The regulatory changes removed the option of compensating analysts for work related tospecificinvestment banking transactions, which eliminated one important potential conflict of interest.6However, analysts are part of a broader organization and the regulatory changes do not prevent analyst compensation from being dependent on the success of theoverallorganization. Analyst report disclosures suggest that analyst compensation may continue to depend on investment banking revenues in the post-Settlement period. For instance, a Morgan Stanley analyst report indicates: “The equity research analysts principally responsible for the preparation of Morgan Stanley research have received compensation based upon various factors, including quality of research, investor client feedback, stock picking, competitive factors, firm revenues and overall investment banking revenues.”7Therefore, regulations adopted in 2002 are not expected to completely eliminate strategic analyst forecasting behavior given that (a) sell-side research has traditionally been seen as a cost-center for financial institutions and that the proportion of the overall organization’s revenue derived from investment banking significantly exceeds that from the brokerage business for many institutions,8and (b) the investment banking arm of an analyst’s brokerage house continues to seek compensation for investment banking activities from coverage firms as indicated by the “Research Disclosure” section of analyst reports published in the post-Global Settlement period and investment banks warn investors about the potential conflicts of interest in analyst research after the passage of the Global Settlement.9,10

Before these regulatory changes and enforcement actions, it was typical for analysts to use a five-tier rating system for their stock recommendations, where they had both buy and strong buy recommendations. After 2002, all sanctioned investment firms and most other brokerage houses transition to a coarser three-tier system and investors lose the benefit of a more granular stock rating system (e.g.,Kadan et al. 2009). CorroboratingKadan et al. (2009), we find in panel A ofTable 1a widespread transition from five-tier to three-tier scale rating systems among brokerage houses after 2002. In 2001, only 31.60% of brokers employed a three-tier rating system for stock recommendations and 14.60% of stocks were covered by three-tier brokers. These figures sharply increased to about 60% in 2003 and exceeded 70% from 2006 on. All 10 original sanctioned investment banks that signed the Global Analyst Research Settlement in 2002 (joined by Deutsche Bank and Thomas Weisel in 2004) transition to a coarser three-tier rating system shortly after.

Given that analysts have strong incentives to satisfy institutional client demand for better compensation and career advancement (Brown et al. 2015) and buy recommendations are expected to be the most important for long-only institutional clients, finer granularity among buy recommended stocks allows analysts to distinguish between stocks that they expect to perform well and stocks whose performance they expect to be even better.11Existing evidence shows that the strong buy recommendations have incremental information relative to buy recommendations. For instance,Barber et al. (2001)show that strong buy recommendations have more investment value compared to buy recommendations, andMalmendier and Shanthikumar (2014)find that institutional investors trade in response to strong buy recommendations, but not to buy recommendations. However, potential conflicts of interest may lead to situations in which analysts issue their most positive stock recommendations for strategic reasons, such as increasing the likelihood of their firms being hired as underwriters and supporting revenue-generating activities of investment banking arms or providing booster shots to investment banking clients (seeMehran and Stulz 2007, for an extensive review of the related literature). For instance,James and Karceski (2006)show that analysts affiliated with the lead underwriter of an initial public offering (IPO) used the strong buy recommendation to support the performance of firms that have recently gone public. If finer granularity among buy recommendations was valuable to investors or better enabled analysts to act strategically to support the affiliated investment banking arm, we would expect it to resurface in the post-Global Settlement era.

Consistent with this expectation, we find that brokerage houses extensively use a top pick designation to distinguish their top buy recommended stocks after the Global Settlement. Academic research that evaluates the implications of investment banking conflicts on sell-side analyst stock recommendations in the post-Global Settlement era does not address the question of whether top picks are used for strategic reasons because it does not consider top picks. Hence, the finding that the impact of investment-banking-related conflicts of interest on stock recommendations has decreased or even has been eliminated after the Global Settlement (e.g.,Corwin et al. 2017) does not imply that top picks are not used for strategic reasons.

To construct a database of top picks, we cannot use I/B/E/S because it does not carry information about the top pick designation of stocks covered by analysts and treats all post-regulatory period buy recommendations the same. Following our conversations with sell-side analysts employed at bulge bracket investment banks, we manually construct a sample of top picks fromThomson Reuters InvestextandThomson Reuters Eikonby searching each full-text analyst report for discussions on the variants of “top pick & best idea.”12Overall, we have a sample of 3,563 top picks identified by 113 unique brokers over 1999–2016.

Panel B ofTable 1shows that the Global Analyst Research Settlement is followed by the emergence of and a steady increase in the new top pick designation. The first column shows only 17 top pick firms in total between the years 1999 and 2001. The number of top pick stocks, however, exhibits a steep upward trend in the years following the regulations enacted in 2002. In 2003, there were 49 top pick firms, a number that increases to 128 in 2004, and 200 in 2005. In the last 6 years of our sample period, at least 300 top picks can be identified by analysts each year.

We distinguish brokerage houses by rating scales and find that the majority of top picks are generated by three-tier brokers. For example, in 2016 nearly 98.5% of top picks are issued by brokers with a three-tier rating system. This is potentially consistent with three-tier brokers attempting to increase rating granularity among buy recommended stocks or enable strategic forecasting behavior. Further evidence of this interpretation is shown in column 4 of panel B that displays the fraction of top picks specifically issued by those brokers that switched from a five-tier to a three-tier rating system. For example, in 2004, the year in which top picks increase from 49 top pick firms to 128 top pick firms, 100% of top picks are issued by brokers that switched from a five-tier to three-tier rating system. In 2005 (2006), these switching brokers account for over 98% (95%) of top picks, and this number remains consistently high throughout our sample period with five-tier to three-tier rating transitioning brokers still accounting for over 70% of top picks in 2016. Since each analyst can at most have one top pick (if any) in a year according to industry practice, it is not surprising that top pick firms represent only 0.16% of buy rated stocks by three-tier brokers in 2003, reaching a peak of 1.86% in 2008. As indicated earlier, in sharp contrast to a buy recommendation, a top pick designation is an exceptional distinction for a coverage stock and represents an analyst’s best investment idea. Further, as shown in panel C, 77.86% of top picks are not accompanied by recommendation initiations, revisions, or reiterations. This lack of overlap suggests that we can directly isolate the investment value of top picks and institutional and retail investors’ trading reaction to top pick announcements. In the remainder of our paper, we focus on top pick designations that do not overlap with stock recommendation initiations, revisions, or reiterations.

In panel D ofTable 1, we find that most top picks (80%) are announced from November to March. They are announced for the upcoming one-year investment horizon and the designation typically expires on December 31 of the year for which a stock is designated as a top pick (unless reiterated or removed before its expiration). In contrast, buy recommendations do not exhibit such time clustering, they extend over an unspecified investment horizon, they do not expire at the end of a calendar year, and a sizable fraction of them are neglected (ie, not dropped, revised, or reiterated) by analysts (e.g.,Boulland, Ornthanalai, and Womack 2022;Birru et al. 2022).

Panel E ofTable 1documents analyst and brokerage characteristics across top pick issuing analysts and other analysts.Internet Appendix Table A1provides detailed information on our variable construction. While the differences in analyst- and brokerage-specific characteristics across these groups of analysts are statistically significant, these differences do not appear economically important. For instance, top pick analysts have slightly shorter experience covering the stock compared to other analysts. Moreover, top pick analysts have slightly fewer years of general employment experience, smaller research portfolios (based on the number of coverage firms and industries), and lower likelihood of being an All-Star analyst as well as being employed at a large top-10 brokerage house. Finally, relative to other analysts, top pick analysts are somewhat more likely to be employed by an investment bank.

Finally, in panel F ofTable 1, we adopt a univariate event-day approach and compare the abnormal market reactions to the announcement of top picks and buy recommendations. Our results document that the mean cumulative abnormal DGTW characteristic-adjusted returns (CARs) over the [0, +1] event window surrounding the announcement of analyst research is 2.24% for top picks (column 1) and 1.87% for buy recommendations by the same analyst during the same year (column 2), and this difference is significant at 1%. In the remaining columns, we compare announcement CARs of top picks with those of buy recommendations in the same four-digit Global Industry Classification System (GICS) classification code (Boni and Womack 2006;Bradley, Gokkaya, and Liu 2017) during the same year and continue to find similar results.13Therefore, our evidence shows that stocks, on average, react more strongly to the announcement of top picks compared to that of buy recommendations.
article.2 Characteristics of Top Picks:
In this section, we examine how firm, analyst, and forecast characteristics differ between top picks and stocks with buy recommendations. On the one hand, it is possible that analysts assign top pick designations based on expected future outperformance of top pick stocks. On the other hand, as indicated earlier, analyst compensation continues to be based on overall investment banking revenue in the post-Settlement period and investment-banking-related conflicts of interest may tempt analysts to assign top picks to support revenue-generating activities of affiliated investment banking units.

As a first step toward understanding the potential motives underlying analysts’ choice of top pick firms, we compare stock-level characteristics of top pick firms with buy recommended stocks (seeInternet Appendix Table A2). Relative to buy recommendations, top pick stocks are larger, have higher institutional ownership, higher stock turnover, have greater analyst coverage, and lower idiosyncratic volatility and earnings forecast dispersion. Compared to an analyst’s buy recommendations, they exhibit greater relative EPS optimism and have significantly higher target price implied returns. More importantly, top pick stocks are about 46% more likely to be affiliated with the investment banking unit of the forecasting analyst’s brokerage house and such affiliation is often described in the literature as an indication of potential conflicts of interest. Overall, our univariate analyses suggest that it is plausible that potential investment-banking-related conflicts of interest may affect the selection of top picks and adversely affect the investment performance of top picks.

These univariate comparisons could potentially be misleading, however, as they do not consider any confounding effects. Next, we estimate logistic regression models where the dependent variable is a binary indicator that equals one if stockjis assigned a top pick designation by analystifor yeart, and zero if a stock operates in the same industry, is rated buy in yeart, and does not carry top pick status by analystior any other analyst. We control for a host of analyst-, broker-, and firm-specific characteristics introduced in Section 2 and defined inInternet Appendix Table A1. We further consider two indicator variables that equal one if the forecasting analystior brokerkissued a top pick in the past, zero otherwise (Top pick broker (past), Top pick analyst (past)). Our logistic regressions include industry-year (or analyst-year) paired fixed effects, and we report standard errors that are heteroscedastic consistent and clustered at the firm level.

Model 1 ofTable 2uses industry-year fixed effects and compares the characteristics of top picks with buy recommendations generated in the same industry during the same year. Consistent with the earlier univariate analyses, the coefficient estimates show that top pick stocks tend to be relatively larger and are also more likely to be growth and momentum stocks as measured by the book-to-market ratio and the past 12-month return. Top pick stocks have more institutional investors, but not more analyst coverage. While top picks have lower idiosyncratic volatility and earnings forecast dispersion, they have higher turnover.

Focusing on analyst forecast optimism, the positive coefficient estimates on relative EPS optimism and target price implied returns are consistent with analysts expecting higher EPS and stock returns from top picks compared to buy recommended stocks. For example, a one-standard-deviation increase in% target price implied returnincreases the odds of a stock being designated top pick by 29.9% relative to other buy recommended stocks. Similarly, a one-standard-deviation increase in relative EPS forecast optimism is associated with a 117% increase in the likelihood of a stock being selected as top pick relative to other buy recommended stocks. We also find that analysts are more likely to select investment banking clients as their top picks and the economic significance of investment banking affiliation on top pick selection is substantial. Specifically, investment bank affiliated stocks are associated with a 102% higher likelihood of being designated as top picks relative to buy recommendations. Therefore, at first sight, our evidence suggests that investment-banking-related potential conflicts of interest may compromise top pick selections of sell-side analysts in the post-Settlement period. We also find that the strongest predictor of a top pick designation is whether the forecasting analyst or brokerage house used a top pick designation in the past. This is consistent with the view that if an analyst or a broker uses a top pick designation in the past, they are more likely to use such a designation again. We find that other analyst- or broker-specific characteristics do not vary significantly across top pick firms and buy recommendations. In Models 2 and 3, we focus on analysts issuing at least one top pick and reestimate logistic regressions with the inclusion of analyst-year paired fixed effects. That is, we compare the attributes of top pick stocks to buy recommendations generated by the same analyst in the same year. Our main inferences remain unchanged.

A top pick designation would be uninformative if all that an analyst does is select the buy recommended stock with the highest expected price appreciation as her top pick. To examine this possibility, in Model 3, we identify the ranking of each buy recommended stock in an analyst’s coverage universe in a given year based on its target price implied return and then include a binary indicator variable that equals one for stocks with the highest target price implied return (ie,Target price rank-one). We include further binary indicators for stocks with the second, third, fourth, and fifth highest target price implied stock return in the same analyst’s coverage portfolio in the same year (Target price rank-two/three/four/five). While the stock with the highest target price implied return (Target price rank-one) is more likely to be selected as a top pick relative to other buy rated stocks in an analyst’s portfolio, the coefficient estimate for stocks with the highest target price implied returns is not significantly larger than for stocks ranked 2, 3, and 4. This suggests that analysts do not simply follow a mechanical rule of selecting the stock with the highest target price implied stock return as their top pick, but instead, take into account other considerations when identifying their best ideas. These other considerations may also include potential investment banking conflicts of interest.

The existence of potential investment-banking-related conflicts in the selection of top picks does not necessarily imply that the investment value of top pick selections is adversely affected by these conflicts. Such potential conflicts of interest may even improve the investment value of top picks for at least two reasons. First, analysts with investment banking affiliation to the coverage firm have better access to management (e.g.,Bradley, Gokkaya, and Liu 2020;Cohen, Lou, and Malloy 2023), and hence, affiliated analysts may possess superior information about the stocks they designate as top picks. Second, analysts affiliated with investment banks have better research training and access to enhanced resources, such as research data, administrative support, research associates (Clement 1999;Jacob, Lys, and Neale 1999;Clement and Tse 2005), and superior analyst training, and research resources may improve the investment value of top picks. Next, we will investigate the investment value of top picks.
article.3 Investment Value of Top Picks:
Whether top picks have investment value for investors is an open empirical question. On the one hand, if analysts confer top pick status on stocks for which they expect the best investment performance and analysts possess stock-picking skills in identifying top picks, then we expect the top pick designation to be informative for future returns. On the other hand, if stocks are given top pick status for strategic reasons or analysts do not possess stock-picking skills (e.g.,Altinkiliç, Hansen, and Ye 2016), then we would not expect the investment performance of those top picks to be superior compared to buy recommendations or alternative analyst investment strategies.

As a first step toward providing answers to the question of whether top pick designations have investment value, inFigure 1, we plot cumulative abnormal daily DGTW characteristic-adjusted returns over [–5, +250] trading days relative to the announcement of top picks.Figure 1documents that abnormal stock returns continue to rise in the months following the announcement of top picks. More importantly, there is no reversal in stock returns over the course of the top pick investment horizon, suggesting that the top pick designation is informative about a stock’s fundamental value. Focusing on daily stock returns over the [–5, –1] event window prior to the announcement of top picks, we do not find significant abnormal returns. This is consistent with the notion that analysts do not piggyback their top picks on recently released corporate news, in sharp contrast to piggybacking behavior documented for buy recommendations in past studies (Loh and Stulz 2011;Altinkiliç, Balashov, and Hansen 2013;Bradley et al. 2014).Figure 1also plots cumulative abnormal stock returns to industry-year and analyst-year-matched buy recommendations over [–5, +250] trading days relative to their announcement dates, and documents that top picks significantly outperform these buy recommendations.

3.1 Calendar-time portfolios

To better assess whether top pick designations have superior investment value, we next employ an investor-oriented equal-weighted calendar-time portfolio approach. We followBarber et al. (2006)and construct a portfolio comprised of top picks (theTop Pick Investment Strategy) and a portfolio comprised of analyst-year or industry-year-matched buy recommendations (theBuy Recommendation Investment Strategy), but without a top pick designation. For the investment portfolio of top picks, we start by identifying the announcement date of a top pick designation and then skip a trading day before inclusion into the portfolio to ensure the information on top picks is publicly available to all market participants. For instance, if stockiis announced as a 2016 top pick on January 4th of 2016 by analystj, stockienters the top pick portfolio at the close of the following trading day (ie, January 5th) and exits the calendar-time portfolio at the close of trading on December 31st of 2016. If stockiis reiterated as a 2017 top pick by analystj, then it remains in the top pick portfolio until the close of trading on December 31st of 2017. If analystjremoves the top pick designation on stockibefore its expiration on December 31st of 2016, then stockiremains in the top pick portfolio until the close of trading on the top pick removal date. We then rebalance the top pick portfolio daily when a new top pick is announced or the current top pick designation expires, is reiterated, or removed before its expiration. We calculate abnormal portfolio returns with a multitude of characteristic and risk adjustments, including DGTW characteristic-adjusted returns, risk-adjusted portfolio returns from theFama and French (1993)three-factor model (three-factor alpha), theCarhart (1997)momentum factor model (four-factor alpha), thePastor and Stambaugh (2003)liquidity factor model (five-factor alpha) as well as Fama-French’s short-term and long-term reversal factor models (six- and seven-factor alpha).

To understand the investment value of top picks relative to buy recommendations, we compare returns from theTop pick investment strategyto returns from theBuy recommendation investment strategy. We have two different approaches of forming portfolios that invest in buy recommendations. The first approach, the analyst-year-matched portfolio, uses buy recommendations from the top pick analyst during the same year as the top pick. The second approach, the industry-year-matched portfolio, uses buy recommendations in the same industry as the top pick during the year of the top pick.14For the portfolio of buy recommendations, we follow an analogous methodology to construct the portfolio of top picks except for expiration dates. As indicated earlier, stock recommendations do not expire at the end of a calendar year. Hence, a buy recommendation remains in the portfolio unless analystjrevises the buy recommendation or the buy recommendation becomes stale.15

Panel A ofTable 3presents the results using the analyst-year-matched portfolios. Comparing abnormal returns accrued to top picks with those to buy recommendations generated by the same analyst during the same year, we find that a calendar-time investment portfolio comprised only of analyst top picks generates DGTW-adjusted monthly returns of 1.33% (17.18% annualized), while buy recommendations yield only DGTW-adjusted monthly returns of 0.51% (6.29% annualized). The difference is not only statistically significant at conventional levels (t-statistic for the difference is 3.25) but also economically important. These results are robust to measuring risk-adjusted abnormal returns using the factor models discussed earlier. Therefore, theTop pick investment strategyappears to have significantly greater investment value for financial market participants than aBuy recommendation investment strategy, suggesting that analysts possess stock-picking skills in identifying their top picks.

In panel B, we report results using industry-year-matched buy portfolios. Therefore, we investigate whether top picks’ outperformance extends to buy recommendations in the same four-digit GICS industry classification code during the same year (excluding recommendations of top pick analysts). If a buy recommended stock is designated as a top pick byanotheranalyst in the same industry during the same year, we remove this stock from the portfolio of industry-year-matched buy recommendations throughout the paper. As discussed earlier, analysts characterize top picks as representing their “best idea” among the stocks they cover. We expect theTop pick investment strategyto outperform theBuy recommendation investment strategyconstructed using the same industry-year-matched buy/strong buy recommendations issued by other analystsonly iftop picks also represent the best ideas in an industry. Panel B ofTable 3presents the results. The last column shows that, on average, DGTW-adjusted monthly returns of theTop pick investment strategyare 90 basis points higher than those of theBuy recommendation investment strategythat invests in buy/strong buy recommendations in the same industry and year (t-statistic for the difference is 4.17). The remaining rows of the last column show that the magnitude of differences in abnormal returns between theTop pick investment strategyand theBuy recommendation investment strategyis even larger, ranging between 110 and 120 basis points, when risk adjustments are implemented using factor models.

In panels C and D, we examine the possibility that top picks designations are simply another name for “strong buy” recommendations by brokers transitioning from a five- to a three-tier rating system following the regulatory changes of the early 2000s. Since there can only be one top pick, but an analyst might have multiple strong buys in her coverage portfolio, we would expect that the investment performance of top picks is greater than the investment performance of strong buy recommendations if analysts have stock-picking skills. This also would be the case if conflicts of interest were more of an issue for strong buy recommendations before the regulatory changes than they are for top picks after the regulatory changes. To this end, panel C ofTable 3compares the abnormal returns of theTop pick investment strategyto those of a strategy of investing in pre-tier change era strong buys issued by the same analyst. The pre-tier period sample of stock recommendations starts in 1993 where we require the same analyst to issue a stock recommendation in the pre-tier as well as post-tier change period. The results in panel C show that theTop pick investment strategysubstantially outperforms the strategy of investing in pre-tier change strong buy recommendations by the same analyst.16In panel D ofTable 3, we also compare the abnormal returns of theTop pick investment strategyto abnormal returns of investing pre-tier change era in strong buy and buy recommendations in the same industry and find similar results. In sum, our findings are consistent with top picks having higher investment value than pre-tier change strong buy recommendations, further emphasizing the structural differences between top picks and stock recommendations as discussed in earlier sections.

A logical concern with the industry-matched analyses inTable 3is that analysts identifying top picks may possess superior stock-picking skills relative to analysts not issuing top picks so that our results may be biased by differences in analyst forecasting skills. We do not have such concerns for analyst-year-matched analyses as comparing the abnormal performance of top picks to that of analyst-year buy recommendations effectively controls for differences in observable and unobservable time-variant and time-invariant analyst skills across these two portfolios. To alleviate this concern for industry-matched analyses,Internet Appendix Table A3compares buy recommendations of analysts who use the top pick designation to industry-year-matched buy recommendations of other analysts who do not use such a designation. The abnormal return differences range between 8 and 24 basis points per month depending on characteristic and risk adjustments. However, none of the differences is statistically significant at conventional levels.17,18

3.1.1 Investment delays and lower frequency rebalancing

With our analysis in this section so far, we assume that investors can implement theTop pick investment strategyat the closing price of the day following the top pick announcement. In this section, we investigate whether the investment strategy is still profitable if investors implement it with a longer delay. We first examine in panel A (B) ofTable 4whether the investment strategy continues to outperform theBuy recommendation investment strategyimplemented with analyst-year (industry-year) matched buy recommendations when using a methodology that skips 5 trading days after the announcement of analyst research and buys the stock at the close of dayt+ 6 relative to the announcement date as opposed to the close of dayt+ 1. Excluding the 6 days immediately after the announcement of a top pick does not change our inferences about the superior investment value of top pick designations compared to buy recommendations. For instance, average characteristic-adjusted (seven-factor alpha) abnormal monthly returns for theTop pick investment strategyare 106 (104) basis points after excluding the [0, +5] event window surrounding the announcement of top picks. In contrast, theBuy recommendation investment strategyhas an average abnormal return that is significantly lower and is only 38 (24) basis points.

Next, we show results when skipping 10 or 20 trading days following the announcement of top picks. Entering positions 11 (21) trading days after the announcement of top pick designations reduces the average abnormal return by roughly 37% (43%) relative to the average abnormal return documented inTable 3, but the strategy continues to exhibit economically significant average abnormal returns ranging from 84 to 94 (77 to 89) basis points depending on the risk adjustment. TheTop pick investment strategycontinues to outperform theBuy recommendation investment strategyusing recommendations issued by the same analyst (panel A ofTable 4) or in the same industry (panel B). It is important to note that while the investment performance of theTop pick investment strategyis statistically significant for all factor models used to adjust for risk, the performance of theBuy recommendation investment strategyis insignificant for the vast majority of factor models when skipping 5, 10, or 20 trading days following the announcement of analyst research. This evidence suggests that if an investor invests in top pick stocks or buy recommended stocks with a slight delay, there is strong evidence of investment performance for the strategy of investing in top pick stocks, but almost no evidence of investment performance for buy recommendations. The evidence for buy recommendations highlights the importance of investors acting quickly to generate returns on analyst recommendations as suggested byBarber et al. (2001)andAltinkiliç, Hansen, and Ye (2016).

To assess the investment performance of top pick designations, it is also important to consider whether the investment performance we document could deteriorate or vanish because of transaction costs. Importantly, top picks are released less frequently than other analyst outputs. Consequently, theTop pick investment strategyrequires less frequent portfolio rebalancing than alternative investment strategies based on other analyst outputs, so that our estimates likely understate the superior investment performance of theTop pick investment strategyrelative to these alternative investment strategies. Nevertheless, the results in this section assume that calendar-time portfolios are rebalanced relatively quickly when a new top pick is designated or an existing top pick stock loses its designation.Internet Appendix Table A6shows that the investment performance of theTop pick investment strategyretains statistical and economic significance if we rebalance calendar-time portfolios weekly (panel A) or monthly (panel B) instead of daily. For example,Internet Appendix Table A6documents that changing portfolio rebalancing frequency from daily to weekly or monthly results in statistically significant abnormal returns ranging from 83 to 126 basis points, depending on the risk adjustment. In contrast, average abnormal returns generated by industry-year- and analyst-year-matched buy recommendations range from -11 basis points to 39 basis points depending on the risk adjustment.

3.1.2 Comparison of the top pick investment strategy to alternative analyst investment strategies

Prior research documents that analyst outputs can be used to form portfolio strategies that earn significant abnormal returns. In this section, we examine how the abnormal returns of theTop pick investment strategycompare to the abnormal returns of investment strategies using other analyst outputs identified in the existing literature. In panel A ofTable 5, we report abnormal returns of alternative analyst investment strategies. In panel B ofTable 5, we report the calendar-time spread portfolios where we compare the abnormal returns of alternative analyst investment strategies to that of theTop pick investment strategy.

We first consider alternative analyst investment strategies developed byBarber et al. (2001).Barber et al. (2001)focus on the profitability of calendar-time portfolios formed using consensus analyst stock recommendations and find that stocks with the most favorable consensus recommendations generate significant abnormal returns for financial market participants. FollowingBarber et al. (2001), we compute the average consensus recommendation level of each coverage stock using the average of the numerical ratings issued by all analysts, and then identify stocks with the most favorable consensus stock recommendation level (ie, strong buy and buy). We then compare the investment performance of top picks to that of stocks with the most favorable consensus stock recommendations. We write this investment strategy asAnalyst investment strategy 1.19Table 5documents that theTop pick investment strategygenerates an average abnormal return that is typically more than four times the average abnormal return ofAnalyst investment strategy 1using stocks in the same industry during the same year. For instance, using the seven-factor model, we find that the average abnormal return of theTop pick investment strategyis 1.30% per month while the average abnormal return ofAlternative investment strategy 1is only 0.25% per month (column 1 of panel A). The differences in DGTW-adjusted and risk-adjusted monthly abnormal returns between the two strategies are economically important and statistically significant at conventional levels (column 1 of panel B).

Barber et al. (2001)also show that forming calendar-time portfolios solely with stocksupgradedto the most favorable recommendation level can generate significant abnormal returns. Hence, we followBarber et al. (2001)and form calendar-time portfolios that include only stocks upgraded to buy and strong buy recommendations. We write this investment strategyAnalyst investment strategy 2and present the results in column 2 of panel A ofTable 5. Once again, we find superior performance for theTop pick investment strategyrelative to this alternative investment strategy (panel B, column 2).20

Huang, Mian, and Sankaraguruswamy (2009)show that combining the information in consensus stock recommendation levels and price targets may generate even larger average abnormal returns. We use this approach to form calendar-time portfolios forAnalyst investment strategy 3. The portfolio strategy invests in the stocks fromAnalyst investment strategy 1that have a consensus target price implied stock return (TPIR) in the top quintile of TPIRs. To calculate TPIR, we divide each analyst’s target price by the stock price prevailing on the day prior to the announcement of a target price and then subtract one from this ratio. The consensus TPIR is calculated as the mean of TPIR across all analysts following the stock. We show results forAnalyst investment strategy 3in column 3 of panel A ofTable 5. Consistent with earlier results, theTop Pick investment strategyoutperforms this alternative investment strategy (column 3 of panel B). In column 4 of panel A ofTable 5, we show results forAnalyst investment strategy 4that uses the stocks inAnalyst investment strategy 2(ie, stocks upgraded to buy and strong buy recommendations) that are also in the highest quintile of consensus implied target prices. Once again, theTop pick investment strategyhas superior investment results (column 4 of panel B).

Barber et al. (2006)show that an investment strategy that combines information about stock recommendation revisions with information about a broker’s stock rating distribution generates superior average abnormal returns. We implement this investment strategy asAnalyst investment strategy 5in column 5 of panel A inTable 5. More specifically, we rank brokers each month in ascending order of the percentage of recommendations that are buys in montht−1⁠. The brokers in the lowest quintile of this percentage are those with the least favorable stock recommendation distribution. We then create the calendar-time portfolios by using stocks upgraded to the most favorable recommendation level from brokers with the least favorable recommendation distribution. Panel B ofTable 5shows that the investment performance of theTop pick investment strategyis more than twice the investment performance of theAnalyst investment strategy 5and these differences are statistically significant (column 5). Next, instead of using the brokers with the least favorable stock recommendation distribution, we implement another strategy fromBarber et al. (2006)that consists of stocks upgraded to the most favorable recommendation level only from independent brokers (Analyst investment strategy 6). Independent brokers are defined as brokerage houses without affiliated investment banking arms. Once again, the performance of theTop pick investment strategydominates that ofAnalyst investment strategy 6(column 6 of panels A and B). Lastly,Analyst investment strategy 7constructs a calendar-time portfolio with stocks upgraded to the most favorable recommendation level (fromAnalyst investment strategy 2)by analysts with superior relative earnings forecast performance as inLoh and Mian (2006). To measure relative earnings forecast performance, we use the proportional mean absolute forecast (PMAFE) as inClement (1999). Analysts in the lowest quintile of PMAFE are defined as analysts with superior forecasting ability (lower values of PMAFE represent more accurate earnings forecasts). It is again the case that theTop pick investment strategygenerates economically and statistically greater investment returns (column 7 of panels A and B).

In sum, while alternative analyst investment strategies based on a single analyst output or combination of analyst outputs generally generate significant abnormal returns, an investment strategy based solely on top pick designations generates superior returns for financial market participants compared to strategies that use analyst outputs considered in prior research.

3.1.3 Regression evidence

InTable 6, we use the panel regression methodology adopted by past related work (e.g.,Cohen, Frazzini, and Malloy 2010) to ensure our results are not driven by uncontrolled firm-, analyst-, and broker-specific characteristics. We use daily DGTW-adjusted abnormal returns as the dependent variable, but convert coefficient estimates into monthly abnormal returns for ease of interpretation. Analogous to our baseline calendar-time portfolio methodology, we exclude the trading day of the top pick designation or buy recommendation announcement. Our key independent variable of interest isTop pick, which is an indicator variable equal to one if a stockjis given top pick status by analystifor yeart. As in earlier sections, we control for a battery of analyst-, broker-, and firm-specific characteristics. Regressions include combinations of year-month, industry-year, and analyst-year fixed effects.

Model 1 ofTable 6includes only buy recommendations issued by the top pick analyst in the same year and reports regression results with analyst-year fixed effects. The coefficient estimate onTop pickis positive and significant, suggesting that top picks outperform buy recommendations issued by the same analyst during the same year. Model 2 focuses only on buy recommendations in the same industry and in the same year as top picks and includes industry-year paired fixed effects to investigate whether top pick stocks’ outperformance extends to industry-year-matched buy/strong buy recommendations generated byotheranalysts. The positive and significant coefficient estimate onTop pickcorroborates the earlier results. In economic terms, top pick stocks yield monthly abnormal DGTW-adjusted returns that are 0.84 percentage points higher compared to industry-year-matched stock recommendations of other analysts. Model 3 (4) repeats the analyses in Model 1 (2) after excluding stock returns between dayt+ 1 andt+ 5 and find robust results. InInternet Appendix Table A9, we reestimate Models 1 through 4 ofTable 6but exclude stock returns followingt+ 10 days (Models 1 and 3) and followingt+ 20 days (Models 2 and 4) relative to the announcement of analyst research. Once again, we find robust results.

In Models 5–8, we compare the performance of top picks to buy recommendations issued by the same analyst (in the same industry) during the pre-tier-change period. The coefficient estimate from Model 5 (6) onTop picksuggests that top picks outperform pre-tier buy recommendations generated by the same analyst (in the same industry) by roughly 1.34% (0.86%). Moreover, the coefficient estimates onStrong buyindicate that top picks exhibit statistically and economically significant outperformance relative to strong buys issued by the same analyst and in the same industry in the pre-tier-change period, lending further support to the evidence presented in panels C and D ofTable 3. Results remain robust when we exclude returns between dayt+ 1 andt+ 5 in columns 7 and 8.

Finally, we employ an analogous panel regression approach to investigate whether top picks have superior returns compared to the various alternative analyst investment strategies considered in Section 3.1.2. To this end,Internet Appendix Table A11adds seven key binary indicator variables of interest that equal one if a buy recommended stock belongs to the earlier discussed alternative analyst investment strategies 1 through 7 (Analyst investment strategy-one/two/three/four/five/six/seven), and zero otherwise. Across Models 1 through 7, the coefficient forTop pickis positive and statistically significant at the 1% level. Comparing the coefficient forTop pickto that of alternative analyst investment strategies, we continue to find that top picks possess economically and statistically greater abnormal investment value for financial market participants.

Overall, the evidence from this section suggests that top pick designations not only generate economically important and statistically significant abnormal returns, but they also outperform buy recommendations and these effects are not subsumed by analyst, broker, and firm-level covariates. Furthermore, top pick designations do not simply reflect renaming of pre-tier change period “strong buy” recommendations. Therefore, these findings are consistent with top picks,on average, reflecting analysts’ best ideas in the post-regulation era and analysts possessing skill in identifying top picks.21

3.2 Cross-sectional analyses: Investment bank affiliation, forecast optimism, analyst, and broker characteristics

The evidence in Section 3.1 indicates that top picks possess superior investment value. It is nevertheless possible that strategic considerations exist for a subset of top pick stocks and such considerations adversely affect the investment value of those top picks. Separately, if top picks are a manifestation of an analyst’s ability to identify stocks with superior investment value, then we would expect the investment value of top picks to exhibit cross-sectional variation related to forecasting optimism, analyst- or broker- specific characteristics. To better assess the first question of whether strategic considerations influence top picks, we examine the extent to which the investment value of top picks varies cross-sectionally based on whether the top pick stock is an investment banking client. We then assess whether target price and relative EPS forecast optimism on top pick stocks is related to the investment value of top picks. Lastly, we investigate how analyst- and brokerage-specific characteristics are related to top pick performance.

Because strategic considerations are likely greatest for investment bank affiliated stocks, we augment the panel regressions inTable 6with an interaction between top picks and investment banking affiliation. Models 1 and 2 ofTable 7show that while the coefficients for the interaction terms between top pick and investment banking affiliation are negative (Top pick*Investment bank affiliation), these coefficients are statistically and economically insignificant. These results suggest that even though analysts are more likely to designate top pick status to investment banking clients (seeTable 2), such top picks do not significantly underperform top picks designated on nonclient firms. Hence, the proposition that investment-banking-related strategic considerations systematically erode the investment value of top picks is not supported by the data.

Next, we investigate whether the investment value of top picks varies cross-sectionally with the forecasting optimism of the analyst on top pick stocks. We compute optimism for earnings forecasts as well as for target price estimates. If forecasting optimism is somehow a manifestation of strategic considerations or analyst behavioral biases (e.g.,Cen, Hilary, and Wei 2013;Jannati et al. 2022), then we would expect the investment performance of top picks to be negatively related to forecasting optimism on top pick designations. Alternatively, if optimism reflects genuine beliefs of analysts on the future stock performance of top picks and they have forecasting skill, we would expect top picks accompanied by more optimistic earnings and target price estimates to have higher investment value. Evidence from columns 3 and 4 ofTable 7is consistent with the latter view.

In Models 5 through 8 ofTable 7, we examine whether top pick investment performance varies cross-sectionally with analyst reputation, analyst forecasting experience, and brokerage house resources. To the extent that analysts with better reputation and longer forecasting experience have a better understanding of their industries and generate more valuable recommendations (e.g.,Cohen, Frazzini, and Malloy 2010;Harford et al. 2019), we expect top picks from such analysts to outperform top picks by other analysts.

To test this conjecture, Model 5 ofTable 7interacts top picks with the forecasting analyst’s All-Star status and finds that top picks selected by All-Star analysts indeed have superior investment performance relative to top picks selected by other analysts. In economic terms, monthly DGTW-adjusted abnormal returns accrued to top picks are 0.69% higher when the designation is made by an All-Star analyst. In Model 6, we turn our attention to analyst forecasting experience. We define analyst forecasting experience as the total number of years that an analyst has appeared in I/B/E/S at the time of announcement of an analyst output. We then create an indicator variable that equals one for analysts with above sample median forecasting experience, and zero otherwise (Analyst long general experience).Interacting top pick status with this indicator, we find that longer analyst experience is also associated with greater investment value for top picks. Model 7 replaces general analyst experience with analyst firm-specific experience where such experience is calculated as the number of years that the analyst appears in I/B/E/S covering the stock at the time of announcement of analyst output (Analyst long firm experience). Strong evidence indicates that top picks by analysts with longer firm-specific forecasting experience have greater investment value (Top pick*Analyst long firm experience) relative to other top picks.

Finally, we analyze the association between the investment value of top picks and brokerage house resources. Past work documents that large brokerage houses take advantage of economies of scale and provide their analysts superior resources compared to other brokers. As a result, analysts at larger brokers may generate higher quality investment research outputs (e.g.,Clement 1999;Jacob, Lys, and Neale 1999;Bradley, Gokkaya, and Liu 2017). In Model 8 ofTable 7, we interact top picks with a top-10 brokerage house indicator and do not find significant evidence consistent with better broker resources improving the investment value of top picks. Across each of these specifications, the parameter estimate onTop pickcontinues to be statistically and economically significant.
article.4 Who Trades on Top Picks?:
In this section, we first examine whether institutional and retail investors trade on top pick designations. We then investigate whether institutional investors and retail investors can distinguish among top picks based on their investment value.

4.1 Institutional versus retail trading behavior

We start by investigating whether institutional investors trade in response to top pick announcements. We rely on 286 million daily equity transactions executed by 886 unique funds over the 2000 to 2014 period obtained fromAncerno LtdInstitutions inAncerno Ltdaccount for roughly 10% of all institutional trading volume and contains institutional trades by mutual funds, pension funds and hedge funds (Puckett and Yan 2011;Ljungqvist and Qian 2016;Jame 2018). We calculate the total institutional trading imbalance for the funds in theAncerno Ltddata set (institutional trading imbalance) as the difference between the number of shares bought and sold by institutions over the [0, +1] event window surrounding the announcement date of top pick designations and buy recommendations divided by the number of shares outstanding for the same stockjat timet.22Total institutional trading imbalance is then scaled by its standard deviation in the pre-event window ([–30, –120 trading days relative to the announcement of analyst research). Next, we reestimate the regressions from Section 3.1.3 with the total institutional trading imbalance serving as our dependent variable.t-statistics are Newey-West adjusted with five lags throughoutTable 8. Model 1 and 2 of panel A inTable 8show the institutional buy-sell trading imbalance obtained fromAncerno Ltdis significantly higher for top picks relative to buy recommendations. Economically, Model 1 shows top picks are associated with a 8.73% higher institutional trading imbalance compared to buy recommendations generated in the same industry for the same year. In Model 2, we find that top picks are accompanied by 10.87% higher institutional trading imbalance compared to buy recommendations generated by the same analyst for the same year.
