Next, we turn our attention to retail investors. We identify retail trading from daily Trade and Quote (TAQ) data as inBoehmer et al. (2021)andBusse, Green, and Jegadeesh (2012), among others. These papers take advantage of two institutional features of retail trading: (a) the majority of stock trades by retail investors takes place off-exchange (filled from broker’s investors or sold to wholesalers) and is classified by TAQ using an exchange code “D,” and (b) retail trades receive very small price improvements relative to the National Best Bid or Offer (ranging between 0.01 cents to 0.2 cents). We identify transactions as retail purchases (sales) if a trade is executed just below (above) a round penny. To be conservative, we omit trades executed at a round penny or near half-penny. Finally, similar to institutional trading imbalance, we define retail order trading imbalance as the difference between the number of shares bought and sold by retail investors divided by the number of shares outstanding for stockjat timet. Once again, the retail trading imbalance is scaled by its standard deviation in the pre-event window ([–30, –120] trading days relative to the announcement of analyst research). Regulation National Market System established the current regulatory framework for subpenny stock price improvement in January 2005. Hence, our analyses of retail trading behavior start in 2005. We reestimate the regressions from panel A ofTable 8with total retail order imbalance over the [0, +1] event window serving as our dependent variable.

Consistent with the evidence presented for institutional investors, retail traders also seem to exhibit more pronounced buying behavior around the announcement of top picks. For instance, Model 1 of panel B inTable 8shows that the retail trading imbalance is 28.42% higher for top picks compared to buy recommendations in the same industry for the same year. In Model 2, we find that retail trading imbalance is 45.72% higher for top picks relative to buy recommendations by the same analyst for the same year. Given the superior investment value of top picks documented in Section 3, the evidence of significantly higher trading imbalance for institutional and retail investors is suggestive of both groups of investors trading on analyst top picks.23

4.2 Do institutional and retail investors distinguish among top picks?

The evidence in Section 4.1 showing increased buy-sell trading imbalance coupled with the earlier evidence showing that an average top pick has investment value suggests that both institutional and retail investors may benefit from top picks. To better understand the extent to which institutional and retail investors may benefit from top picks, we next explore their ability to discern among top picks based on their investment value when top picks are announced. We first focus on the ability of institutional investors to distinguish better top picks from worse top picks, and then turn to retail investors.

Institutional investors represent the most important constituency for analyst research. The academic literature examines whether institutions can sort through Wall Street research and discern good and bad stock recommendations; however, the evidence is mixed at best. For example,Malmendier and Shanthikumar (2007)andMikhail, Walther, and Willis (2007), among others, suggest institutions only act on good stock recommendations and ignore uninformative ones. Conversely,Busse, Green, and Jegadeesh (2012)do not find evidence of these investors possessing superior skills to analyze and discern among stock recommendations.

Analysts’ top picks provide a unique and important laboratory to isolate institutional investors’ ability to distinguish among analyst research outputs for at least two reasons: (1) top picks capture significantly higher attention from institutions relative to buy recommendations (seeInternet Appendix Table A13), and (2) analysts typically have one-on-one calls with institutional investors or buy-side analysts to provide additional explanations on the investment theses and conviction behind their top pick selections shortly after their public announcements. Given the significant efforts made by analysts to further communicate and explain their top picks to institutional investors, we expect institutional investors to analyze and discern among top picks based on their investment value.

In panel A ofTable 9, we augment our earlier investment value panel regressions from Section 3.1.3. with the addition of a variable capturing institutional trade imbalance and its interaction with top pick designation. Model 1 uses the institutional trading imbalance over the [0, +1] event window relative to announcement of analyst research. When constructing our dependent variable of investment value, we skip 5 trading days before including top picks into the portfolio to mitigate potential concerns that institutional buying (selling) pressure following the announcement of analyst research may bias our inferences. In other words, if stockjis announced as a 2016 top pick on January 4, 2016, by analysti, stockjenters the top pick portfolio at the close of dayt+ 6 (ie, January 12th). The positive and significant coefficient estimate on the trade imbalance variable (Institutional trade imbalance (0, +1)) indicates that analyst outputs that are accompanied by higher institutional trade imbalances exhibit higher investment value, consistent with the conclusions ofMalmendier and Shanthikumar (2007)andMikhail, Walther, and Willis (2007). More importantly, the coefficient for the interaction term (Top pick* Institutional trade imbalance (0, +1)) is positive and statistically significant, indicating that top pick announcements with higher institutional trade imbalances exhibit higher investment value than the average top pick. In Models 2 through 4, we focus on institutional trade imbalance over the [0, +2], [0, +3], and [0, +4] event window relative to announcement of top picks and continue to find similar results. Models 5 through 8 repeat the analysis with industry-year-matched samples. Once again, results are robust. Overall, our results provide strong empirical support for the notion that institutions are not only more likely to trade on top picks but also capable of discerning among top picks based on their investment value.

In panel B, we repeat analogous regressions for retail investors. Unlike institutional investors, retail traders are typically less sophisticated and often have a relationship only with one investment advisor or broker. As such, it is potentially more difficult and costlier for retail traders to distinguish between good and bad analyst research when it is announced.24In our context, we examine whether retail investors take all top picks at face value or discern among top picks based on their investment value. Examining this association is particularly relevant given that the SEC advises retail investors “do their homework before investing” in a company solely because of its “top pick” status.25Panel B ofTable 9suggests that retail investors do not distinguish between good and bad top picks. In particular, the coefficient for the interaction term is insignificant in all specifications. The evidence indicates that retail investors do not appear to discern between good and bad top picks, but they can generate abnormal returns from trading on top picks because on average they have investment value.
article.5 Conclusion:
In this paper, we show that analysts make frequent use of the top pick designation after the regulatory changes and the Global Analyst Research Settlement of 2002. Shortly after the regulatory changes, many brokerage houses move to a three-tier rating system that reduces the granularity of the information provided to investors compared to the five-tier system prevalent before 2002. The top pick designation enables analysts to provide greater granularity of information to investors among buy recommended stocks within the three-tier rating system. It is used to highlight an analyst’s best investment idea. We find that, on average, this designation has economically and statistically significant investment value for investors. We also show that both institutional investors and retail investors trade in response to a stock receiving a top pick designation.

The obvious issue with granularity of information is that it makes it possible for analysts to draw more attention to specific stocks in a way that can be highly valuable to the firms that receive that attention. Analysts might therefore be tempted to use this top pick designation to pursue strategic objectives (ie, support affiliated investment banking business) other than giving the best investment advice to investors. The three-tier system is largely viewed as a way to reduce the value of this discretion for analysts. Absent the temptation of analysts to use a valuable designation to pursue strategic objectives that are not in the best interest of investors, greater granularity among buy recommended stocks is especially valuable to investors. Institutional investors, the primary consumers of sell-side research, are mostly long-only investors, so their ability to benefit from negative information is much more limited than their ability to benefit from positive information. Hence, greater granularity among buy recommendations allows analysts to differentiate between stocks that are expected to perform well and those whose performance is expected to be even better. We investigate whether analysts use the top pick designation strategically. We find that, on average, they do not in that investors gain substantially from following top picks.

We show that an investment strategy of investing in top picks dominates an investment strategy of investing in buy recommendations, and also dominates investment strategies proposed in the literature that make use of various analyst outputs. The economic benefit from investing in top picks appears to be quite large. Using investor-oriented calendar-time portfolios, we find that the monthly abnormal return of investing in top picks exceeds 130 basis points per month for six different approaches of measuring abnormal returns. We find no evidence that top picks designated on investment banking clients perform worse than other top picks, consistent with the view that potential conflicts of interest do not adversely affect the investment value of top picks. Our further cross-sectional analyses document that top pick designations accompanied by more optimistic earnings and target price forecasts have greater investment value, and so do the top picks of analysts with greater reputation and longer forecasting experience.

After showing that investors on average can generate abnormal returns from investing in top picks and that the abnormal returns from doing so exceed those from alternative investment strategies that use analyst outputs, we show that both institutional and retail investors trade in response to top pick designation announcements. We also find that the top pick designations that are associated with greater trading by institutional investors perform better, so that it appears that institutional investors have some ability to identify better performing top picks. In contrast, there is no relation between a top pick’s performance and the trading behavior of retail investors.

1 Kadan et al. (2009) argue that the shift to a three-tier rating system is driven by the new regulations requiring brokers to disclose the percentage of all securities rated buy, hold, and sell. In addition to a neutral rating, a three-tier rating system includes only one optimistic (e.g., buy) and one pessimistic rating option (e.g., sell). In contrast, a five-tier rating system includes two categories to denote optimism (e.g., strong buy/buy) and two categories to denote pessimism (e.g., strong sell/sell).

For instance, an institutional client stated about Roderick Bourgeois that “his top pick proved to be solid gold in a time when most stocks utterly tanked.” Internet Appendix Table A13 documents that top picks garner significantly greater institutional and retail attention compared to buy recommendations when they are announced.

This is not surprising since top picks typically represent only less than 1% of buy recommended stocks at any point in time (see panel B of Table 1). The composition of investment portfolios based on alternative analyst outputs is recalculated every day, because an analyst may change or drop her recommendation for a coverage firm, or a recommendation may become stale. Any required portfolio rebalancing occurs at the end of each trading day. Similar to the calendar-time portfolio comprised only of top picks, stocks enter the alternative analyst investment strategy calendar-time portfolios at the close of the trading day following the announcement of their inclusion into a corresponding Analyst Investment Strategy portfolio.

21 A plausible concern is that analysts choose top picks because they are in the news. In this case, the investment performance of top picks could be due to an underreaction to news rather than stock-picking skill of the analyst. We investigate this possibility when the top pick overlaps with news. As already discussed, such a situation is unusual. Nevertheless, we investigate this situation. We create an indicator variable that equals one for analyst research overlapping with news announcements, zero otherwise. We then add this indicator variable and its interaction with Top picks to Model 2 of Table 6. Internet Appendix Table A10 documents that the coefficient for this interaction term is insignificant ( t -statistic of 0.379), indicating that top picks that are accompanied by news do not generate statistically significantly different investment returns compared to top picks that do not overlap with news.

22 Internet Appendix Table A12 considers institutional and retail trading imbalance over the alternative windows [0, +2], [0, +3], and [0, +4]. Our inferences from Table 8 remain similar.

23 In Internet Appendix Table A13 , we further investigate whether institutional and retail investors also pay attention to top pick announcements. To this end, we measure institutional investors’ attention with search activity on Bloomberg terminals, and then transform Bloomberg’s score using a truncated normal distribution so that the Bloomberg search scores take the value of –0.350, 1.045, 1.409, 1.647, and 2.154, respectively (e.g., Ben-Rephael, Da, and Israelsen 2017 ; Focke, Ruenzi, and Ungeheuer 2020 ). To measure the attention of retail investors, we follow related work and focus on the average Google Search Volume Index (GSVI) and abnormal AGSVI that subtracts the median value of GSVI over the 8 weeks preceding the announcement of analyst research. To make the data collection and screening process more manageable, we restrict the institutional and retail attention analysis to S&P 500 firms. Our results show that both institutional and retail investors appear to devote more attention to the announcement of top pick designations relative to the announcement of industry-year or analyst-year matched buy recommendations over [0, +5] event window surrounding the announcement of analyst research.
